intro: Apricot is grafted onto whatever suits the ground, and in Australia that is usually a peach seedling. In the temperate stone-fruit districts apricots are budded onto peach rootstocks such as Nemaguard and Golden Queen, which have wide compatibility and like deep, free-draining soil. Where the soil is heavy or wet a plum rootstock (Myrobalan or Marianna) is used instead, and the size-controlling Citation gives a smaller tree. You can also raise your own from an apricot stone, because an apricot seedling is compatible with any apricot.
sources_note: Rootstock guidance on this page draws on the FAO review of Australian deciduous fruit production, university foundation stock records, the Dave Wilson Nursery grower comparisons, and Australian and international nursery rootstock references.

sources.name: FAO: deciduous fruit production in Australia (regional rootstock tables)
Dave Wilson Nursery: grower rootstock comparisons
Cultivar: rootstock for apricots, peaches and plums
Foundation Plant Services, UC Davis: Myrobalan 29C rootstock record
Silver Creek Nursery: plum and apricot rootstock (Myrobalan 29C)
The Fruit House: fruit tree rootstock compatibility

rootstocks.name: Peach seedling (Nemaguard, Golden Queen)
rootstocks.soils: Deep, well-drained sandy soils; poor on heavy or wet ground
rootstocks.notes: The usual apricot rootstock in Australia's temperate stone-fruit districts, and the widest-compatibility stone-fruit stock: peach seedlings take apricots, peaches, nectarines and plums alike. They make a vigorous tree and want free-draining ground, so they are the wrong pick for heavy or wet soils.

rootstocks.name: Myrobalan (and Myro 29C)
rootstocks.soils: Medium to heavy soils; good tolerance of waterlogging
rootstocks.notes: The plum rootstock for apricots on heavier or wetter ground: Myrobalan is commonly used for apricots and tolerates waterlogging and medium-to-heavy soils, but it is poor with peach and nectarine, and some apricot varieties show delayed graft compatibility, so match the stock to the variety.

rootstocks.name: Marianna (GF8-1, 2624)
rootstocks.type: Plum hybrid (P. cerasifera x P. munsoniana)
rootstocks.notes: A plum hybrid for heavy, poorly drained ground. It is used for apricot in California, but reports of apricot compatibility vary (some sources rate it good, others rate most apricots incompatible), so treat it as a plum-first stock. It is very susceptible to bacterial canker and incompatible with peaches and nectarines.

rootstocks.name: Citation
rootstocks.notes: The size-controlling option: highly compatible with apricot and plum, tolerant of wet soils, and it brings the tree into bearing early. The trade-off is susceptibility to crown gall, bacterial canker and oak root fungus.

grow_your_own.intro: Apricot is one you can raise from a stone. An apricot seedling is compatible with any apricot, so a stone from a good apricot makes a serviceable home rootstock, though in Australia most apricots are actually worked on a peach or plum stock instead.

sections.heading: Apricot from a stone
sections.body: A stone from a good apricot grows into an apricot seedling (Prunus armeniaca) that is compatible with all apricots, because it is the same species. Clean and stratify the stone as you would a peach, sow it in spring, and bud your chosen variety onto the seedling once it reaches pencil thickness. Expect a vigorous, full-size tree, and give it deep, well-drained soil.

sections.heading: Why Australian apricots are usually on peach or plum
sections.body: In practice, apricots in Australia are commonly budded onto peach seedlings (Nemaguard, and peach varieties such as Golden Queen) in the temperate stone-fruit districts, because peach rootstocks have the widest compatibility and suit deep, free-draining soils. Where the ground is heavy or wet, a plum rootstock such as Myrobalan is used instead, since it tolerates the waterlogging that a peach stock cannot.

faqs.q: Can I grow an apricot rootstock from a stone?
faqs.a: Yes. An apricot stone grows into an apricot seedling (Prunus armeniaca), which is compatible with all apricots because it is the same species, so it makes a sound home rootstock. Expect a vigorous, full-size tree, and give it deep, well-drained soil. In practice many Australian apricots are grown on peach seedlings instead, which have wide compatibility, or on a plum rootstock such as Myrobalan where the ground is heavy or wet.

faqs.q: Which rootstock suits an apricot on heavy or wet soil?
faqs.a: A plum rootstock is the answer on heavy or poorly drained ground. Myrobalan tolerates waterlogging and medium-to-heavy soils and grafts well with apricot, and Marianna copes with wet, heavy soil, though it is very susceptible to bacterial canker and its compatibility with some apricots is less certain. On free-draining ground a peach seedling, or the size-controlling Citation, which is highly compatible with apricot and also wet-tolerant, are the better choices.
